caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: From: Universal-International, Universal City, Calif. "You Gotta Stay Happy" -- A Pictorial Synopsis. Bringing Joan Fontaine and James Stewart together for the first time, "You Gotta Stay Happy" is the second picture made by Rampart Productions for Universal-International. Rampart is owned by Joan and her husband, William Dozier. The picture was adapted from Robert Carson's Saturday Evening Post serial by Producer-writer Karl Tunberg and is thoroughly screwball, one of the gayest comedies of the year. It also is the first comedy Joan has made in four years, has last being "The Affairs of Susan." The film was directed by H. C. Potter. The story in pictures: 7. Making a forced landing in a pasture during a storm, Payne, Dee Dee, Bullets and passenger Caslon (Porter Hall), who completes the strange cargo, are put up by farmer Racknell (Percy Kilbride) where they witness the strange spectacle of the Racknells saying good night to their myriad youngsters.

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen and Richard Todd in London after filming Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: During the filming of Happy-Go-Lovely at Elstree studios, Hollywood dancing star Vera-Ellen had no chance of looking round London. She was either rehearsing or before the cameras every day of the production. So when at last, after five months hard work, her part in the film was completed Vera-Ellen decided to see the famous landmarks of England's capital for herself. Her guide and companion for the day was Britain's top male star, Richard (Hasty Heart) Todd, who was himself sightseeing in Hollywood only a few months earlier. Together, the two made a lightning tour of the city, visiting such well-known spots as Piccadilly Circus, Trafalgar Square, Fleet Street, Green Park and the Houses of Parliament. On the steps of the Eros statue in Piccadilly Circus Richard Todd buys a carnation from one of London's traditional flower-sellers and pins it on Vera-Ellen's jacket.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen, David Niven, and extras in Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Dancing together at the Bon Viveur Restaurant, Edinburgh, are multimillionaire B.G. Bruno (David Niven) and Janet Jones (Vera-Ellen), leading lady at the Mercury Theatre. Both are the dinner guests of John Frost, who is producing the show in which Janet is appearing. Through a misunderstanding, however, Janet believes that Bruno is actually Paul Tracy, a newspaper reporter. Happy-Go-Lovely is an Associated British-Marcel Hellman Technicolor production starring David Niven, Vera-Ellen and Cesar Romero with Bobby Howes and Diane Hart. Bruce Humberstone directs this lavish backstage musical story which is set against a background of the famous Edinburgh Festival.

title_tesim: Vera-Ellen in England after shooting Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Hollywood dancing star Vera-Ellen, tired after five months of hard work at Elstree studios, recently spent a day in the country for relaxation and pleasure. Her first stop was the charming old village of Cookham, in Berkshire, where she lunch at the famous fifteenth-century hotel, the Bel and the Dragon. Here she is seen examining an ancient hand-made Swiss clock in the lounge of the hotel. Vera-Ellen co-stars with David Niven and Cesar Romero in the Associated British-Marcel Hellman Technicolor production Happy-Go-Lovely, directed by Bruce Humberstone.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Cesar Romero and other cast members in Happy-Go-Lovely
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: John Frost (Cesar Romero) faces his creditors from the stage of the Mercury Theatre, Edinburgh. Frost is the produce of a show called Frolics to You, but he has not sufficient money to let the show open. The creditors demand the amount that is owing to them, and in reply Frost lets them watch the opening number of the show - Mackintosh's Wedding. The creditors finally agree to give him forty-eight hours in which to raise the money. Happy-Go-Lovely is an Associated British-Marcel Hellman Technicolor production starring David Niven, Vera-Ellen and Cesar Romero and directed by Bruce Humberstone.

title_tesim: Mickey Rooney and Tina Thayer in A Yank At Eton
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: MICKEY ROONEY'S NEW SCREEN SWEETHEART...Mickey Rooney gets himself a brand new sweetheart on the screen in Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer's 'A Yank at Eton." She is red-haired Tina Thayer, who stands just five feet tall. Born in Worcester, Mass., she appeared in several plays in New York before going to Hollywood on a vacation. After two small movie roles had extended her "holiday," she landed a role with Mickey in the new picture, directed by Norman Taurog, produced by John W. Consadine, Jr. 1. SHE LEARNS WHAT THE WELL-DRESSED YOUNG ETONIAN WILL WEAR...And in excess, as Mickey is caught running errands for older Eton students in one scene of the picture.

title_tesim: Harold Lloyd and extras in Professor, Beware!
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: From Small Beginnings-- Many of the cinema's brightest stars of today had obscure beginnings. Some few have leaped at once into prominence. The roles of nearly all have varied from picture to picture. It is quite a contrast to consider first roles and present ones of the players in question. Memories are reawakened in many instances of pictures of yesteryear, and of some personalities long forgotten. 2) More than over tops in the field of thrills, Harold Lloyd packs many spine-tinglers in his latest picture Professor, Beware!, together with numerous comedy quirks. Here is one scene from his new picture. Harold's in trouble, as might be expected.

title_tesim: Dick Powell and Peggy Dow in a publicity still for You Never Can Tell
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: From: U-I, Universal City, Calif. Dick "Speaks" For His Dinner In "You Never Can Tell." Don't feel sorry for Dick Powell - just because he leads a "dog's life" in Universal-International's bright new comedy hit, "You Never Can Tell." Lovely Peggy Dow is Dick's co-star and "master" in the hilarious film which casts Powell in the role of a police dog who is murdered then returns to earth as a Private Eye to track down his murderer. Dick and Peggy are assisted in the madcap comedy by Charles Drake and Joyce Holden, the latter a reincarnated racing filly.

title_tesim: Mickey Finn, Yul Brynner, Charles Boyer, and other cast members in The Buccaneer
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: The Battle of New Orleans -- On January 8, 1815 was fought one of the most famous battles in United States history. It ended the War of 1812. This battle is portrayed in Paramount's epic action picture, "The Buccaneer," a Techni-color and VistaVision multi-million dollar production which stars Yul Brynner, Charlton Heston, Claire Bloom, Charles Boyer, Inger Stevens and Henry Hill. Jackson, with a makeshift army of 3,200 men, was out-manned four to one when the fabulous pirate, Jean Lafitte, turned from outlaw to pirate when he rejected an offer to earn wealth and standing to fight for the British and chose to lead his band of pirates into battle with Jackson in return for only the right to be American citizens. He brought Jackson desperately needed kegs of powder and 7,500 rifle flintlocks. The battle was fought in a thick fog and the British made use of rockets which they fired from large tubes. The flaming, screaming rockets were mainly used to strike terror in the minds of Jackson's motley army. Brynner plays Lafitte; Heston plays Jackson in the film. Boyer portrays Dominique You, pirate, who became an artillery hero that day. When that battle was finished, the British losses included 858 killed, 2,468 wounded. Jackson counted his own losses: 8 killed, 14 wounded. The Paramount picture, "The Buccaneer," was produced by Henry Wilcoxon, directed by Anthony Quinn, supervisied by Cecil B. DeMille.From the thick swamps come a long line of pirates, bearing powder and weapons and led by Jean Lafitte (Yul Brynner) and Dominique You (Charles Boyer).
"The Buccaneer"Produced by Henry Wilcoxon Directed by Anthony Quinn Supervised by Cecil B. DeMilleA Paramount Picture In VistaVision  Color by Technicolor

title_tesim: Ella Raines, Virginia Brissac, and Fay Helm in Phantom Lady
caption_tesim: "PHANTOM LADY""Phantom Lady," a tense mystery drama, is a Universal production starring Franchot Tone, Ella Raines and Alan Curtis, directed by Robert Siodmak with Joan Harrison as associate producer. It is the story of a young man unjustly condemned to die for the murder of his wife, of the despairing efforts of the girl who loves him, to locate the "phantom" witness who can establish his innocence, and of the mad machinations of the real criminal in seeking to save himself from exposure.12. Carol finally learns the identity of the "phantom Lady" as a young socialite, Ann Terry (FAY HELM), and in high spirits rushes to her estate, only to find that Miss Terry is a mental patient, under care of Dr. Chase (VIRGINIA BRISSAC).From: Universal Studios,Universal City, Calif."Phantom Lady," a tense mystery drama, is a Universal production starring Franchot Tone, Ella Raines and Alan Curtis, directed by Robert Siodmak with Joan Harrison as associate producer. It is the story of a young man unjustly condemned to die for the murder of his wife, of the despairing efforts of the girl who loves him, to locate the "phantom" witness who can establish his innocence, and of the mad machinations of the real criminal in seeking to save himself from exposure.12. Carol finally learns the identity of the "phantom Lady" as a young socialite, Ann Terry (FAY HELM), and in high spirits rushes to her estate, only to find that Miss Terry is a mental patient, under care of Dr. Chase (VIRGINIA BRISSAC).

title_tesim: Inger Stevens and other cast members in The Buccaneer
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Pirate's Kingdom Destroyed -- A highlight of Paramount's "The Buccaneer" depicts a real-life dramatic episode from the colorful life of the fabluous pirate, Jean Lafitte, played in the production by Yul Brynner. Lafitte, a man without a country, had left his pirate stronghold of Barataria, in swamplands near New Orleans, to offer the services of hsi pirates in the fight against the invading British. He wants only the right to live under the American flag. He and one of his captains, played by George Mathews, are riding home in a parogue, when they find their little kingdom completely destroyed. Lafitte is sure than the British, whom he has been intending to double-cross, have done the job. But Claire Bloom, a pirate's daughter who has managed to hide out when all the pirates have either been captured or fled into the swamps, bitterly tells Lafitte that it was Americans who have betrayed Lafitte. In spite of this, Lafitte did in real life lead his pirates into battle to help General Andrew Jackson defeat the British at the Battle of New Orleans on January 8, 1815. Other stars in the picture are Charlton Heston, Charles Boyer, and Inger Stevens. The picture, filmed in Technicolor and VistaVision, was produced by Henry Wilcoxon, directed by Anthony Quinn and supervised by Cecil B. DeMille.Leading citizens of New Orleans attend the reception for General Andrew Jackson (Charlton Heston) in the home of Governor Claiborne (E.G. Marshall). Annette (Inger Stevens), the governor's daughter, sings a song for the general.

title_tesim: Robert F. Simon and cast members in a scene from The Buccaneer
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: The Battle of New Orleans -- On January 8, 1815 was fought one of the most famous battles in United States history. It ended the War of 1812. This battle is portrayed in Paramount's epic action picture, "The Buccaneer," a Techni-color and VistaVision multi-million dollar production which stars Yul Brynner, Charlton Heston, Claire Bloom, Charles Boyer, Inger Stevens and Henry Hill. Jackson, with a makeshift army of 3,200 men, was out-manned four to one when the fabulous pirate, Jean Lafitte, turned from outlaw to pirate when he rejected an offer to earn wealth and standing to fight for the British and chose to lead his band of pirates into battle with Jackson in return for only the right to be American citizens. He brought Jackson desperately needed kegs of powder and 7,500 rifle flintlocks. The battle was fought in a thick fog and the British made use of rockets which they fired from large tubes. The flaming, screaming rockets were mainly used to strike terror in the minds of Jackson's motley army. Brynner plays Lafitte; Heston plays Jackson in the film. Boyer portrays Dominique You, pirate, who became an artillery hero that day. When that battle was finished, the British losses included 858 killed, 2,468 wounded. Jackson counted his own losses: 8 killed, 14 wounded. The Paramount picture, "The Buccaneer," was produced by Henry Wilcoxon, directed by Anthony Quinn, supervisied by Cecil B. DeMille.Led by bagpipers and drums to throw fear into the Americans through the sound of the strange music, the Highland troops of the British move toward Jackson's lines.
"The Buccaneer"Produced by Henry Wilcoxon  Directed by Anthony Quinn  Supervised by Cecil B. DeMilleA Paramount Picture  In VistaVision  Color by Technicolor

title_tesim: Ginger Rogers in The Barkleys of Broadway
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: "The Barkleys of Broadway" -- Fred Astaire and Ginger Rogers, the screen's most famous dancing partners, are back together again in Metro-Goldwyn-Mayer's "The Barkleys of Broadway." The new musical, their first in Technicolor, is a gay romantic story of present-day Broadway, highlighted by six big singing and dancing numbers. Oscar Levant, Billie Burke, Gale Robbins and Jacques Francois are featured. Charles Walters directed, Arthur Freed produced. Robert Alton directed the musical numbers.12. While Josh continues alone in their musical comedy, Dinah scores a big hit in her drama, although experiencing difficulties throughout rehearsals. What she doesn't know is that Josh's coaching, when he assumes Jacques' voice on the telephone, has been responsible for her overcoming her troubles.

title_tesim: Robert Warwick, Charles Boyer and other cast members in a scene from The Buccaneer
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: Pirates Meet in Prison -- Paramount's filmization of "The Buccaneer" includes many colorful and dramatic scenes, among which is a historical meeting in the New Orleans prison betewen Jean Lafitte, played by Yul Brynner, and Dominique You, portrayed by Charles Boyer. The action is a page lifted from history. Early in Janury, 1815, the pirate Lafitte bargained with General Andrew Jackson for the release of his buccaneers from prison in exchange fro badly-needed gunpowder and flint with which to halt the advence of British troops. Dominique You was Lafitte's "general" and, at the time of the imprisonment, Lafitte's men had lost faith in him. Lafitte takes the pardon papers to Dominique and then goes off to become a hero by fighting with Jackson. Dominique and the pirates also engaged in the Battle of New Orleans with Jackson and the enemy was routed. The picture also stars Charlton Heston, Claire Bloom, Inger Stevens, Henry Hull and E.G. Marshall. Henry Wilcoxon produced, Anthony Quinn directed and Cecil B. DeMille supervised. The picture was filmed in Technicolor and VistaVision.Dominique You (Charles Boyer), in what he believes is a farewell speech to fellow pirates, says, "There's only one thing we can trust in life -- and that is that it will end in death. When it comes, spit in its eye."
"The Buccaneer"Produced by Henry Wilcoxon  Directed by Anthony Quinn  Supervised by Cecil B. DeMilleA Paramount Picture  In VistaVision  Color by Technicolor

title_tesim: Frank Iwanaga, Raymond Burr and cast members in Godzilla, King of the Monsters!
uniform_title_tesim: Godzilla, King Of The Monsters  (Motion picture)
caption_tesim: Handwritten in purple ink text from back of photograph: 1
Stamped and handwritten in black ink text from back of photograph:Theater Arts LibraryMPS-341-11
Handwritten in black ink text from back of photograph: Gojira (Godzilla, King of the Monsters)
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
description_tesim: "Gojira," the original title of the Japanese film released on November 3, 1954, was produced by Tomoyuki Tanaka and directed by Ishirō Honda. It was distributed in Japan by Toho Company, Ltd. About a year later, American director and editor, Terry Morse, along with producers Harold Ross and Richard Kay, re-edited the film and added scenes shot with American actors, most notably Raymond Burr, and intercut these scenes into the original film. This more 'Western-audience friendly' version of the "Gojira" was retitled, "Godzilla, King of the Monsters!," and released on April 27, 1956 in the U.S.A., distributed by Embassy Pictures Corp. This photograph, featuring Frank Iwanaga and Raymond Burr, was taken from a scene in the 1956 U.S. version of the film. After gaining worldwide popularity as a sci-fi/horror movie icon, 'Godzilla' spawned dozens of film sequels and remakes in the decades that followed.

title_tesim: Charles Drake, Albert Sharpe, Peggy Dow and Dick Powell in a scene from You Never Can Tell
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: From: Universal-International, Universal City, Calif. "You Never Can Tell" --- A Pictorial Synopsis; "You Never Can Tell" is a movie for those who think they have seen every thing. It is literally the doggondest comedy ever filmed, telling a story of the reincarnation of animals as humans. Starring Dick Powell and Peggy Dow, it is from a story by Lou Breslow, screenplay by Breslow and David Chandler, and was directed by Breslow. It was produced by Leonard Goldstein for Universal-International. The story in pictures: 9. Taking Max to Ellen's residence, where Ellen's sea-faring grandpa (Albert Sharpe) has arrived to give her away in marriage, Rex shows Ellen that Max recognizes - and hates - Collins, too. Collins is the villain who had broken King's leg in service, framed a phony jeep accident to hide it, and was dismissed from the K-9 corps for cruelty.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Joey Walsh in Hans Christian Andersen.
caption_tesim: Joey Walsh is, in reality, going to correspondence school while making "Hans Christian Andersen." His lesson outlines and study contents are mailed out each week from New York by the Children's Professional School, a famous school for theatrical moppets (the one that Mildred Dunnock headed.) Samuel Goldwyn hired Jean Seaman here to handle this end of it. He's winding up the eighth grade now and in June will be graduated from junior High School. Joey insisted that he not sever his relations with the Children's Professional School because he wants to stay in the class with his schoolmates, ones he's been with since the first grade. Among them are Iris Mann, the little girl that was so good in "Room For One More" with Cary Grant, and Clifford Sales of the "Date With Judy Show." SG-6800-P-56/ SG-6800-P-55
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: David Wayne and Tom Ewell in Up Front
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph:From: Universal-InternationalUniversal City, Calif."UP FRONT" COMES TO THE SCREENThe Movies Discover the "Dogfaces," Willie & JoeFunniest by-product of World War II was the "dogface," the lowly G.I. whose beefing typifies the democratic civilian-soldier at war. When Bill Mauldin, soldier-cartoonist, personified the "dogface" in the unforgettable Willie and Joe in newspapers and in his book, "Up Front," it was only a question of time until that book reached the screen. After a wide search for actors who would not only look like Willie and Joe, but who could present them in the underplayed, undramatic way of the Mauldin originals, Universal-International has completed "Up Front" starring David Wayne and Tom Ewell. Here they are as seen in the film, borrowing situations and dialogue from the Mauldin cartoons:4 "...so Archibald kissed her agin an' gently put her head on th' pillow. She gazed at him wit' half-shut eyes--tremblin' hard--don't forget to buy next week's installment at yer newsstand."4 "...so Archibald kissed her agin an' gently put her head on th' pillow. She gazed at him wit' half-shut eyes--tremblin' hard--don't forget to buy next week's installment at yer newsstand."

The belt is narrow and embroidered in gold and pink pearls. The full skirt is over a pink gauze that resembles an East Indian Sari. title_tesim: Dennis O'Keefe and Helen Walker in Her Adventurous Night
caption_tesim: Text from back of photo: She Meant No Harm. Dennis O'Keefe and Helen Walker are paired romantically in "She Meant No Harm," rollicking comedy about a telephone trouble-shooter who picks up a girl bound for the altar with another man. Before the final reel, Dennis changes Helen's mind about marrying the other fellow. Crisp dialogue adds to this Universal film's gay mood.

title_tesim: Joyce Holden, Charles Drake and Peggy Dow behind the scenes of You Never Can Tell
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph: From: U-I, Universal City, Calif. "Joyce Hits Winner's Circle as Filly Fashion Plate" Joyce Holden, complete with a purse designed like an oat bag, a jockey-type straw hat with little pointed feathers hinting at ears, offers the ultimate in filly fashions. But it's all in keeping with Joyce's role in Universal-International's new comedy hit, "You Never Can Tell," in which the actress plays the role of a deceased racing filly who returns to earth as a blonde secretary. Also, from animal heaven, comes Dick Powell as a Private Eye out to get the culprit who murdered him WHEN HE WAS A DOG! Peggy Dow, starred with Powell, and Charles Drake admire Joyce's eardrobe.
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections

title_tesim: Gene Tierney and Bruce Cabot in Sundown
caption_tesim: Text from back of photograph:(EXCLUSIVE)WANGER PHOTO by Donald Biddle KeyesGENE TIERNEY - BRUCE CABOTGene Tierney reaches independent stardom and Bruce Cabot plays the romantic male lead in Walter Wanger's spectacular African adventure, "Sundown", soon to be seen on the nation's screens. "Sundown" deals with the exciting experiences of six white men in an isolated colonial outpost along the Kenya-Abyssinia_Jubaland border in Central east Africa. Cabot is the British commissioner. Miss Tierney is Zia, supposedly half-caste daughter of a wealthy Arab trader, who returns to her home just as Shenzi tribesmen, given guns by enemy agents threaten the post as a challenge to British rule. "Sundown" is regarded as one of the the most colorful and distinctive action pictures in years; more suspenseful and arresting than Wanger's memorable 1940 hit, "Foreign Correspondent".
repository_tesim: University of California, Los Angeles. Library. Performing Arts Special Collections
